attribution.name: PlayfulProcess
attribution.note: Generated from research/people dossiers; for maintainer + Tarot History Forum review.

name: People & Institutions of Tarot — The Hands Behind the Cards
description: The makers, patrons, occultists, scholars, and institutions behind the historical tarot decks in this collection. Each node links to the deck(s) that person or body made, reframed, printed, or holds — and, where specific, to the cards that feature them (e.g. Pamela Colman Smith's scenes in the RWS deck). Generated from the research/people dossiers, which carry the sourced evidence.

metadata.image_credit: Arthur Edward Waite portrait — Wikimedia Commons (public domain)

sections.Who:
**Arthur Edward Waite** (2 October **1857** – 19 May **1942**), known as **A. E. Waite**, was an
American-born British mystic and scholar of the occult, a Golden Dawn initiate who became the
**directing mind of the Rider-Waite-Smith tarot**. In 1909 he commissioned the artist **Pamela
Colman Smith** to execute a "rectified" tarot pack to his specifications, and in 1910/1911 he
published *The Pictorial Key to the Tarot* as its companion text. The essential precision: **Waite
designed the symbolic *scheme* and wrote the book — he did not draw the cards.** That work was
Smith's [@web_pkt_wp; @decker2002; @waite1910].
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Common conflation: "the Rider-Waite deck" as Waite's solo creation.** **The record:** Waite
  authored the *programme*; **Pamela Colman Smith drew every one of the 78 cards.** The very name
  the collection uses — **Rider-Waite-*Smith*** — exists to correct a century of crediting Waite
  (and the publisher Rider) while erasing the artist [@web_pcs_wp; @web_rws_wopc].
- **Myth: the deck encodes recovered ancient wisdom.** **The record:** its symbolism is a
  knowing **Golden-Dawn-era construction**, popularised for a general audience; its "antiquity" is
  inherited rhetoric, not pedigree [@decker2002].
sections.In this collection:
Within `golden-dawn-book-t-tarot`, Waite is the **scheme/author node** for the RWS imagery the
grammar borrows: the designer of the symbolic programme and author of its reading book. His leaf
must state plainly that he **designed but did not draw** the cards, pairing him with Smith on the
art and pointing to Mathers/the Order for the underlying correspondences.

metadata.image_credit: Probable self-portrait, Andrea Mantegna — Wikimedia Commons (public domain)

sections.Who:
Andrea Mantegna was one of the foremost painters of the early Italian Renaissance, court
artist to the Gonzaga in Mantua. His name survives in tarot history only through an
**error**: the set of 50 engravings long called the **"Mantegna Tarocchi"** was neither
engraved by Mantegna nor a tarot. The collection catalogues that deck honestly under that
traditional-but-wrong name, then debunks it.
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Myth:** Andrea Mantegna engraved a "tarot" of 50 cards.
- **Record:** The **"Mantegna Tarocchi"** (c. 1465, two series — the earlier *E-series* and a
  copy *S-series*) is a didactic set of **50 engravings in five decades** (estates of man,
  Apollo and the Muses, the liberal arts, the genii/virtues and sciences, the planets and
  spheres) — **a ladder of being, not a card game**, and **not by Mantegna**. It is attributed
  to an anonymous Ferrarese or Bolognese hand (the "Master of the E-series"). The Mantegna
  label is a traditional convenience, repeatedly rejected by scholarship [@dummett1980;
  @web_mantegna_wopc; @web_mantegna_wp].
sections.In this collection:
As the **debunked** namesake of `mantegna-tarocchi`. The deck grammar keeps the familiar name
for findability but states plainly that it is neither Mantegna's nor a tarot. See
`research/decks/mantegna-tarocchi.md`.

sections.Who:
**Bonifacio Bembo** was a mid-15th-century painter from a family of artists active in
**Cremona** and **Milan**, documented in the service of the Sforza dukes from roughly the
late 1440s into the 1470s [@web_bembo_wp]. He matters to tarot because the great
hand-painted **Visconti-Sforza** luxury packs — the headwaters of surviving tarot — are
**traditionally attributed to Bembo or his workshop** [@web_bembo_wp; @web_met_bembo;
@web_wopc_vs]. The attribution is **load-bearing but not secure**: the rival candidate is
the **Zavattari** workshop (Francesco Zavattari), and even Bembo's wider oeuvre has been
questioned by art historians [@web_vs_wp; @web_bembo_wp]. Treat "Bembo" as the conventional
label for the painter(s) behind these decks, not a settled fact.
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **The traditional claim — "painted by Bonifacio Bembo."** This is the attribution
  enshrined by **Gertrude Moakley**, whose 1966 study is literally titled *The Tarot Cards
  Painted by Bonifacio Bembo for the Visconti-Sforza Family* [@moakley1966]. It originates
  earlier: **Roberto Longhi** proposed Bembo in 1928, and the attribution was adopted by
  Wittgens (1936) and Rasmo (1939) [@web_vs_wp].
- **The rival claim — the Zavattari workshop.** **Giuliana Algeri** argued that the
  Brambilla and Visconti-Sforza packs were painted instead by **Francesco Zavattari**, on
  the grounds of stylistic resemblance to the Zavattari frescoes in the Chapel of
  Theodelinda at **Monza** [@web_vs_wp]. The Metropolitan Museum hedges by cataloguing its
  cards under **"Workshop of Bonifacio Bembo"** rather than Bembo's own hand
  [@web_met_bembo].
- **The honest position:** the cards are firmly **Milanese court work of the mid-15th
  century**, but the *individual hand* is genuinely debated. State the debate; do not assert
  Bembo as fact [@web_vs_wp; @web_bembo_wp].
sections.In this collection:
Bembo is the credited **maker** for two deck slugs: **`visconti-sforza-tarot`** and
**`cary-yale-visconti-tarot`**. Wherever either deck's authorship is named, the grammar
should render it as *attributed to the workshop of Bonifacio Bembo (Zavattari proposed as
an alternative)* — never as a flat "by Bembo." He is the maker-node that links to the
`visconti-sforza-patrons` family and to `gertrude-moakley`.

items.name: Louis-Raphaël-Lucrèce de Fayolle, comte de Mellet

sections.Who:
The **Comte de Mellet** — full name **Louis-Raphaël-Lucrèce de Fayolle** (1727–1804) — wrote
the *second*, often-overlooked tarot essay bundled into Court de Gébelin's *Le Monde primitif*,
vol. 8 (1781). While Gébelin supplied the Egyptian *origin myth*, it was **Mellet** who first
in print (a) explicitly paired the **22 trumps-plus-Fool with the 22 letters of the Hebrew
alphabet**, (b) read the trumps in an **inverted order** (from the World down to the Fool) as
the Three Ages of the World, and (c) described a concrete **divination method** — arguably the
oldest published tarot *spread*. He is the quiet hinge between Gébelin's theory and Etteilla's
practice [@decker1996; @web_place_mellet; @web_hrmtc_first].
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Myth: the Hebrew-letter correspondence is an ancient transmission.** **The record:** it is
  an **18th-century construction**. Mellet *proposed* the 22-trumps ↔ 22-Hebrew-letters pairing
  in 1781 as an interpretive overlay on a 15th-century Italian card game; there is no medieval
  Kabbalistic tarot behind it. The idea is influential and internally elegant, but it is
  **invented in print by Mellet (and Gébelin's circle)**, not recovered [@decker1996;
  @dummett1980].
- **Misattribution of the "reading-the-cards" idea to Gébelin.** Popular accounts credit Court
  de Gébelin with founding tarot divination. The record: the *method* and the *Hebrew/divination*
  material are largely **Mellet's**, appended to Gébelin's origin essay; the two should not be
  merged [@web_place_mellet; @decker1996].
- **Identity once disputed.** "M. le C. de M." was for a time mysterious; the consensus
  identification with the comte de Mellet (de Fayolle) is now standard but still worth treating
  as well-supported rather than incontrovertible (confidence: medium-high on the identity)
  [@web_place_mellet; @web_mellet_search].
sections.In this collection:
Mellet makes no deck, so he carries no deck slug; in the grammar he is a **person/idea node**,
paired with **`court-de-gebelin`** on the 1781 origin of the occult reading. He should feature
wherever the collection narrates the **Hebrew-letter correspondence** and the **first tarot
spread** — i.e. as the under-credited author behind a system usually attributed to Gébelin or
Lévi. A "people of tarot" card for Mellet should foreground "the man who first paired the 22
trumps with the 22 Hebrew letters, in 1781."

metadata.image_credit: Antoine Court de Gebelin portrait — Wikimedia Commons (public domain)

sections.Who:
**Antoine Court de Gébelin** (c. 1725 – 1784) is the hinge of the entire "occult turn." In
**1781**, in volume 8 of his vast encyclopedia *Le Monde primitif*, the Protestant pastor and
Freemason published an essay declaring the Tarot de Marseille a survival of the ancient
Egyptian **Book of Thoth**. He offered **no evidence**, and was **wrong** — but he converted a
card game into a "sacred text" and launched two centuries of esoteric tarot. Bundled with his
essay was a second piece by the **Comte de Mellet**, which first proposed actually *reading*
the cards and tying them to the Hebrew alphabet [@decker1996; @web_wopc_monde].
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Myth: tarot is the ancient Egyptian "Book of Thoth."** **The record: FALSE.** Tarot is a
  15th-century North-Italian invention (the *trionfi* / tarocchi card game, first documented in
  the courts of Milan and Ferrara). Gébelin wrote in 1781, **four decades before Champollion
  deciphered hieroglyphs** (Lettre à M. Dacier, 1822); his "Egyptian" reading was pure
  imagination projected onto Italian Christian imagery. The verdict of the document-first
  scholarship is unequivocal: the Egyptian genealogy is **invented, not recovered**
  [@dummett1980; @decker1996].
- **Myth: he recovered "suppressed" esoteric knowledge.** **The record:** he announced his
  theory *openly*, under his own name, in a prestigious subscription encyclopedia read by the
  Parisian elite. The occult overlay was born not in secrecy but as a **high-status
  Enlightenment fad** — fashionable erudition about an imagined Egypt, not a transmitted
  tradition [@decker1996].
- **Important caveat.** Debunking Gébelin removes a false *pedigree*; it does **not** debunk the
  real, later history of divinatory practice (Etteilla onward) or its persecuted practitioners.
  Separate the false antiquity from the genuine 18th-century practice [@decker1996].
sections.In this collection:
Gébelin is the maker of the deck slug **`court-de-gebelin-tarot`** (the 1781 redrawn plates),
which the grammar should treat strictly as **origin-myth**, dated 1781, and **never** as
evidence about the cards' real 15th-century origin. In a "people of tarot" set he is the figure
on whom the **myth-vs-record** split is most load-bearing: state the Egyptian claim, then the
Dummett-spine correction, on his card and on any card descended from his plates (esp. those he
re-Egyptianised). He pairs naturally with the **comte-de-mellet** card as the two halves of the
1781 turn.

metadata.image_credit: Eliphas Levi portrait — Wikimedia Commons (public domain)

sections.Who:
**Éliphas Lévi** — born **Alphonse-Louis Constant** (8 February 1810 – 31 May 1875) — is the
hinge of the *modern* occult tarot. In *Dogme et rituel de la haute magie* (two volumes,
**1854 and 1856**) he was the first to **systematically** weld the **22 tarot trumps to the 22
letters of the Hebrew alphabet** and to the Kabbalah, embedding the tarot in a complete magical
philosophy. He **designed no deck** of his own — but everything downstream (Wirth and de Guaita
in France, the Golden Dawn and Waite–Smith in England) builds on the framework he set. He is the
pivot from the 18th-century salon theorists (Gébelin, Mellet) to the 1889 occult deck
[@decker2002; @tarot_heritage; @levi1856].
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Myth: the tarot–Hebrew–Kabbalah correspondence is an ancient, recovered key.** **The
  record:** Lévi's specific assignment of trumps to Hebrew letters is an **invention of the
  1850s**, an interpretive construction laid over a 15th-century Italian card game — not a
  medieval survival. It is powerful and generative, but its antiquity is asserted, not
  documented [@decker2002; @dummett1980].
- **Myth: "TAROT comes from Egyptian/Hebrew *ROTA*."** **The record:** the ROTA/TARO word-play
  is a mnemonic conceit Lévi popularised, not an etymology supported by the history of the word
  *tarocchi/tarot* [@web_levi_rota; @decker2002].
- **He is honest about being a *builder*, not always about being a *recoverer*.** Unlike the
  flat-false Egyptian-origin claim, Lévi's contribution is genuinely original synthesis; the
  caveat is only that he framed his new system as ancient wisdom. The grammar should record a
  **deliberate, dated esoteric construction (1850s)**, not a transmission [@decker2002].
sections.In this collection:
Lévi carries **no deck slug** (he made no cards) but is a **central person node**: the author
of the correspondence system the esoteric decks rely on. He should feature wherever the
collection narrates the **trump↔Hebrew-letter** mapping and the **Tree of Life** overlay — and
specifically behind the **Wheel of Fortune** (his ROTA/TARO play), the **Devil/Baphomet**
imagery, and as the named source "restored" by the **`oswald-wirth-tarot`** deck. A "people of
tarot" card should frame him as **the man who made tarot Kabbalistic — in the 1850s, by
construction.**

sections.Who:
The **Este dynasty** ruled Ferrara from 1240 to 1597 and were among the most important
**early tarot patrons** in documented history. Their court at Ferrara was a centre of
**Quattrocento humanist culture** — the same milieu that produced the Mantegna Tarocchi
and the Ferrarese style of luxury card-making — and the **Este tarot** (c. 1441–1461)
is named for them. A **1457 Este inventory** lists tarot cards among the court's
possessions, providing one of the earliest dated references to tarot in any archival
source [@dummett1980; @web_este_wp].
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Common framing**: "Tarot was invented in Ferrara." **The record**: Ferrara is the
  site of some of the **earliest dated references** to tarot trump cards in archival
  sources, alongside Milan. Both cities have strong claims. Dummett argues for a Milanese
  or Ferrarese origin in the 1420s–1440s; Pratesi's archival work reinforces the early
  Ferrarese evidence. Neither city can claim exclusive invention from the surviving record.
- **The Este tarot attribution**: the surviving cards are attributed to the Este court by
  conventional scholarship but the specific patron (Leonello? Borso?) and date
  (c.1441–1461?) remain uncertain [@dummett1980].

metadata.image_credit: Jean-Baptiste Alliette (Etteilla) portrait — Wikimedia Commons (public domain)

sections.Who:
**Jean-Baptiste Alliette** (1 March 1738 – 12 December 1791), who reversed his surname to
sign himself **"Etteilla,"** was the first person known to history to earn his living by
reading cards, the author of the first self-instruction manuals for it, and the maker of the
**first tarot deck purpose-built for divination** (1788–89). Where Court de Gébelin supplied a
*theory* (the cards as an Egyptian "Book of Thoth") and the Comte de Mellet sketched a
*method*, Etteilla industrialised both into a working practice, a printed deck, and a
correspondence *school*. His system outlived him as a 19th-century commercial brand through two
posthumous editions (Etteilla II and III) produced by his followers and later publishers
[@decker1996; @tarot_heritage].
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Myth: the deck transmits ancient Egyptian wisdom (the "Book of Thoth").** Etteilla took
  this framing wholesale from Court de Gébelin (1781) and marketed his deck under it. **The
  record:** the Egyptian pedigree is invented. Tarot is a 15th-century North-Italian card game;
  hieroglyphs were undeciphered until Champollion (1820s). Etteilla *founded a real and durable
  divinatory practice* but sold it under a *false antiquity* — both are true, and the dossier
  honours the practice while flagging the pedigree [@decker1996; @dummett1980].
- **Myth: "the first tarot fortune-teller is ancient/timeless."** **The record:** the
  documentary trail for systematic tarot divination begins, concretely, with Etteilla's printed
  books (1770 for cards, 1783–85 for tarot) and his 1788–89 deck — a dated 18th-century
  invention, not a recovered tradition [@decker1996].
- **Etteilla II and III are *not* his work.** They are posthumous continuations by his school
  and by later commercial publishers (Blocquel/"Orsini"; Régamey/Lequart-Mignot/Grimaud).
  Attributing them to Alliette himself is a common error [@web_grand_etteilla; @web_druidcraft].
sections.In this collection:
Etteilla is the maker behind three deck slugs in this repo: **`etteilla-i-livre-de-thot`**
(his own 1788–89 deck), **`etteilla-ii-egyptian`**, and **`etteilla-iii-oracle-des-dames`**
(posthumous). He should feature prominently on the cards his system *renumbered or invented* —
above all the **querent/significator and element cards** that have no Marseille parent, and the
**Death** card (renumbered away from XIII), where his departure from the Marseille tradition is
sharpest. Any "people of tarot" card should frame him as **the first professional cartomancer**
and the bridge from Gébelin's theory to a working deck.

metadata.image_credit: Pisanello, Codex Vallardi 2484 — Wikimedia Commons (public domain)

sections.Who:
**Filippo Maria Visconti** was the **third and last Visconti Duke of Milan** (r. 1412–1447),
son of Gian Galeazzo Visconti who built the dynasty to its greatest extent. He is the
**patron behind the earliest documented tarot trump programme**: his secretary
**Marziano da Tortona** designed a bespoke sixteen-trump deck for him, and the luxury
hand-painted Visconti tarots — though made after his death for the Sforza who succeeded
him — belong to the world his court created [@dummett1980; @pratesi_marziano].
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Common assumption**: "The Visconti tarot" was made for Filippo Maria Visconti.
- **The precise record**: The surviving luxury packs (Visconti-Sforza, Cary-Yale) are more
  plausibly dated **after 1450** and associated with the Sforza who succeeded him. The
  Cary-Yale deck is sometimes linked to the **1441 marriage** of his daughter Bianca Maria
  to Francesco Sforza. Filippo Maria himself commissioned the earlier *Marziano* trump
  series, which did not survive physically.
- **Scholarly consensus** (Dummett, Pratesi): Filippo Maria is the earliest documented
  tarot patron, but the surviving luxury decks are Sforza-era objects, not his.

sections.Who:
**Franco Pratesi** is a retired Florentine physicist who became one of the most
important **archival researchers in tarot history** — not through synthesis or theory,
but through direct excavation of **Italian Renaissance account books, guild records,
and inventories**. His discoveries in Florentine, Ferrarese, and Bolognese archives
have repeatedly pushed back or complicated the accepted chronology of early tarot,
and his work has been essential to the careers of every subsequent tarot historian
[@pratesi_marziano; @pratesi_ferrara; @web_pratesi_site].
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Pratesi's contributions are primary research, not interpretation**: he finds documents;
  others interpret them. Where Dummett or other historians make interpretive claims about
  tarot origins, Pratesi's documents are often the evidence base — but the interpretive
  conclusions are not always Pratesi's own.
- **His findings sometimes complicate neat narratives**: Florentine evidence alongside
  Milanese and Ferrarese evidence suggests tarot spread quickly across multiple Italian
  courts in the 1440s–1460s, making any single "origin city" claim overly simple.

sections.Who:
A cataloguer at the New York Public Library for some four decades, Gertrude Moakley produced
the decisive iconographic insight of modern tarot history: the 22 trumps are a **procession
of triumphs** in the manner of **Petrarch's _I Trionfi_** and the carnival *trionfo* — Love,
Chastity, Death, Fame, Time, Eternity — not a coded Egyptian or kabbalistic book. Her reading
grounded the trumps in the actual visual culture of 15th-century Italy.
sections.Claim vs the record:
Moakley **dissolved** the occult origin-myth on iconographic grounds before Dummett
systematised the documentary case: the trumps are intelligible as Renaissance triumph-imagery,
so no Egyptian or Hebrew key is required to explain them. Her attribution to **Bonifacio
Bembo** is itself now hedged (the Zavattari workshop is a rival candidate) — preserve the
hedge.
sections.In this collection:
Not a maker — the **iconographer**. Cited in the Visconti-Sforza and Cary-Yale dossiers and
in the trump-meaning notes wherever the "triumph procession" reading is invoked.

items.name: Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn

sections.Who:
The **Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn** is the single most important engine of the modern
esoteric tarot. Founded in London on **1 March 1888**, this small, oath-bound magical society
fused tarot, Kabbalah, astrology, alchemy and Hermetic imagery into one graded, internally
consistent system, recorded in a confidential teaching paper called **Book T**. Book T
assigned every card a place in a master grid of **astrological, elemental and decanic
correspondences**, and — to keep that grid tidy — **swapped the traditional numbers of
Strength and Justice (VIII↔XI)**. Crucially, the Order **never published a deck of its own**:
members hand-copied their cards from Mathers' specimen under the secrecy of the lodge. The
*public* realisation of its system came later and from outside the Order's control — the
**Rider-Waite-Smith** deck of 1909 [@decker1996; @decker2002; @tarot_heritage].
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Myth: the Golden Dawn recovered an ancient, suppressed tarot tradition.** **The record:**
  its antiquity is *manufactured* — the founders themselves forged the Sprengel lineage to
  furnish a Continental pedigree. The system is a brilliant **1880s–90s synthesis** drawn from
  Éliphas Lévi, Christian Kabbalah, Renaissance Hermetica and Victorian Egyptology, not a
  transmission [@decker1996; @decker2002].
- **The conflation this collection must correct: "the Golden Dawn deck" ≠ the Rider-Waite-Smith
  deck.** The Order's *own* cards were **unpublished, hand-copied, secret, and now largely lost**.
  The deck nearly everyone calls "the Golden Dawn tarot" in popular usage is in fact the **later,
  public RWS deck** (1909) — a *derivative realisation* of Book T's correspondences by two former
  members, made commercially and outside lodge discipline. The grammar must keep **Book T as
  text** distinct from any **visual deck** [@web_gd_nodeck; @tarot_heritage].
sections.In this collection:
The Golden Dawn is the **institution node** behind the `golden-dawn-book-t-tarot` deck: it
authored the correspondences (titles, Hebrew letters, Tree-of-Life paths, decans) the grammar
renders, while the *imagery* in that grammar is borrowed RWS art. A "people of tarot" leaf
should frame the Order as **the system's author, not its publisher** — and flag that the cards
shown are a later public deck, not the Order's own lost pack.

sections.Who:
Jacquemin Gringonneur is a real but shadowy figure: a Paris painter recorded in a **1392**
royal account paying him **56 sols** for **"three packs of cards, gilded and coloured and
ornamented with various designs, for the amusement of our lord the king"** (Charles VI of
France). Those were **ordinary playing cards**, now lost. His name became famous through a
**19th-century error** that tied this payment to a surviving hand-painted tarot — a deck that
is actually **Italian, c. 1450–80**, not French and not from 1392.
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Myth:** Gringonneur invented/painted the tarot in 1392 for Charles VI (the "Charles VI"
  or "Gringonneur" tarot).
- **Record:** In the mid-19th century a card historian linked the 1392 payment to a set of
  17 hand-painted tarot trumps in the **Bibliothèque nationale de France**. On stylistic
  grounds those cards are **Italian (Ferrara/B-order), c. 1450–80 — about a century too late**
  to be Gringonneur's, and from the wrong country. The deck is misnamed but the name has
  stuck [@web_charlesvi_th; @web_wopc_handpainted; @dummett1980].
sections.In this collection:
As the **debunked** namesake of `charles-vi-tarot`. The deck grammar keeps "Charles VI
(Gringonneur)" for findability and states the real provenance (Ferrarese, c. 1450–80). See
`research/03-charles-vi-gringonneur.mdx`.

metadata.lifespan: fl. Paris, c.1650 (working dates only; birth/death unknown)

sections.Who:
**Jacques Viéville** was a Paris cardmaker active around **1650** — the same city and decade
as **Jean Noblet** — but his tarot is **not** in the Marseille line. It belongs to a
**divergent tradition**, the so-called **"Rouen-Brussels" (Rouen-Bruxelles)** pattern, and is
the French **ancestor of the later Belgian / Flemish tarot** [@web_th_rouen_bruxelles;
@web_belgian_wp]. The only known copy is preserved in the **Bibliothèque nationale de France**
[@web_letarot_vieville]. He is, like Noblet, **thinly documented as a person** — known chiefly
through his deck — so this dossier is short and floruit-only (confidence: medium).
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Claim: "a variant Tarot de Marseille."** **The record:** it is better described as a
  **separate pattern** that happens to be contemporary with the earliest Marseille survivor
  (Noblet). Calling it "Marseille" obscures the Bologna-via-Piedmont ancestry Dummett
  identifies; it is the **Rouen-Brussels / Belgian** line, not the Marseille line
  [@dummett1980; @web_th_rouen_bruxelles].
sections.In this collection:
Viéville is the maker of **`vieville-tarot`**. In the grammar he is the **counter-example to
the Marseille hegemony**: same time and place as Noblet, different lineage. Render the diff
explicitly — *La Foudre*-tree instead of the struck Tower, upright Hanged Man, re-ordered
virtues — and link him forward to the `vandenborre` Belgian deck as the same family's
18th-century continuation.

metadata.lifespan: fl. Paris, c.1650–1681 (working dates only; birth/death unknown)

sections.Who:
**Jean Noblet** was a Paris cardmaker whose pack — surviving in a **single copy in the
Bibliothèque nationale de France** — is the **oldest known near-complete Tarot de
Marseille**, conventionally classed as **"Type I"** [@web_bnf_noblet; @web_wopc_noblet].
Working *"au faubourg St Germain"* around the **mid-17th century (c. 1650; the BnF dates the
surviving deck 1659, and his activity c. 1659–1681)**, Noblet made the earliest object that
conforms to the graphic type later called *tarot de Marseille* because Marseille came to
specialise in it [@web_bnf_noblet]. He is **barely documented as a person** — known mainly
through his name and address printed on the cards themselves — so this dossier stays short
and the biography is floruit-only (confidence: medium).
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Claim: "the oldest Tarot de Marseille."** **The record supports this** with the
  qualifier *surviving*: Noblet's is the oldest **extant near-complete** deck of the Marseille
  graphic type, but the **pattern is named for Marseille only later** (18th c.), and Noblet
  himself worked in **Paris** — so "Marseille" here is a type-label, not a place of origin
  [@web_bnf_noblet; @dummett1980].
- **Type I vs Type II.** Noblet anchors the older **Type I** Marseille; the **Conver 1760**
  deck is the canonical **Type II**. The two-type division is a modern descriptive scheme,
  not a maker's category [@dummett1980; @depaulis1984].
sections.In this collection:
Noblet is the maker of **`noblet-tarot`**, the deck to cite whenever the collection needs
the **oldest surviving Marseille** witness or the **Type I** archetype. In prose, pair him
with `nicolas-conver` (Type I → Type II) and contrast him with `jacques-vieville` (same city
and decade, different pattern). The self-identifying cards — the **Two of Coins / Two of
Cups** (address) and the **Chariot** ("I. N." monogram) — are the ones that physically carry
his signature.

sections.Who:
Leo Aryeh Mayer was an Israeli scholar of Islamic art who, in the 1930s, recognised a set
of richly decorated cards in the Topkapı Sarayı, Istanbul, as a **Mamluk-era playing pack** —
the *Mulūk wa-nuwwāb* ("Kings and Deputies"). His identification supplied the missing link
between Chinese money-suited cards and the European four-suit pack that became tarot.
sections.Claim vs the record:
Mayer's careful description corrected loose talk of "Saracen" or "gypsy" card origins with a
dated, physical object. Note the survivor-count nuance his work and its readers established:
the Topkapı set is **almost complete (~48 cards)**, of which **~43 belong to the original
pack** and a handful are cruder later replacements; the complete pack held 52. (confidence:
high on the structure; the exact survivor count is reported variously as 43/47/48 across
sources — the dossier states the reconciled figure.)
sections.In this collection:
Not a maker — the **identifier** of the `mamluk-deck` ancestor. He is the principal citation
in the Mamluk deck dossier and grammar.

metadata.image_credit: S. L. MacGregor Mathers — Wikimedia Commons (public domain)

sections.Who:
**Samuel Liddell MacGregor Mathers** (8 January **1854** – November **1918**) was the creative
engine of the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn and the **chief author of its tarot teaching,
Book T**. While **Westcott** supplied the Cipher Manuscripts and the order's (forged) pedigree,
Mathers wrote most of the rituals and built the **correspondence system** — Hebrew letters,
Tree-of-Life paths, and the astrological/decanic attributions — that still underlies how almost
everyone reads tarot today. He produced **no published deck**; his cards survived as a lodge
specimen to be hand-copied by initiates [@web_mathers_wp; @decker1996; @tarot_heritage].
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Myth: Book T transmits an ancient Egyptian/Hermetic tarot key.** **The record:** Mathers'
  attributions are a **constructed synthesis of the 1880s–90s**, built on Lévi's trump↔Hebrew
  scheme and the Order's astrology, then projected backward as ancient wisdom [@decker2002].
- **Precision: he authored a *scheme*, not a marketed deck.** Mathers wrote the correspondences
  and painted a lodge specimen; the cards were **hand-copied in secret**, never published. The
  famous *public* cards are Smith's RWS art — a separate, later object [@web_gd_nodeck].
- **The schism candour.** In 1900 Mathers told Florence Farr that **Westcott had forged the
  Sprengel letters** — effectively admitting the Order's foundation legend was fraudulent, even as
  he defended his own authority [@decker1996].
sections.In this collection:
Mathers is the **author node** for `golden-dawn-book-t-tarot`: the titles, Hebrew letters, Tree
paths and decanic meanings rendered in that grammar are his. A "people of tarot" leaf should name
him **the architect of the Book-T correspondences** and stress that he built, did not recover,
them — and that he published no deck.

sections.Who:
**Marziano da Tortona** (fl. early 15th century) was a **humanist secretary** to
**Filippo Maria Visconti**, Duke of Milan, and the **earliest named tarot designer in
history** — perhaps the earliest named designer of any card trump series. Around
**c. 1415–1425** he wrote a Latin treatise, *Tractatus de deificatione sexdecim heroum*,
describing a set of **sixteen trump cards** he had designed for the duke's personal use,
each depicting a Roman god above a bird sacred to that deity [@dummett1980;
@pratesi_marziano; @web_marziano_wp].
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **The claim**: Marziano da Tortona designed the first tarot trumps.
- **The precise record**: He designed *a* trump series — documented, named, classicising —
  that is certainly the **earliest tarot programme we can attach to a named individual**.
  Whether it predates or influenced the standard triumph/trionfi sequence is unknown.
  Franco Pratesi has argued the Marziano deck was made **c. 1418–1425** on the basis of
  manuscript dating [@pratesi_marziano]. Michael Dummett discusses it at length in
  *The Game of Tarot* as evidence for the early Milanese origin of tarot trumps
  [@dummett1980].
- **The key uncertainty**: did the standard tarot trump hierarchy (Fool, Magician, Popess…
  World) derive from experiments like Marziano's, or did both arise independently from a
  broader Milanese court culture of luxury card games? The honest answer is: unknown.

items.name: Master of the E-series (Mantegna Tarocchi engraver)

sections.Who:
The **Master of the E-series** is the conventional name given to the anonymous North
Italian engraver who produced the **earlier and finer set** of the so-called
*Mantegna Tarocchi* — a sequence of **50 didactic engravings** depicting a cosmic
hierarchy from the lowest social estate (the beggar) to the highest sphere (the First
Mover). The name "E-series" comes from the series designation used in early print
catalogues; the companion *S-series* is a slightly later copy, probably by a different
hand [@web_mantegna_wp; @web_mantegna_wopc].

**The attribution to Andrea Mantegna is wrong** — this has been the consensus of art
historians since the early 20th century — but the label persists as a convenient
shorthand.
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **The myth**: Andrea Mantegna engraved this deck. **The record**: rejected by art
  historical consensus. Mantegna's engraving style is known from authenticated works
  (the *Battle of the Sea Gods*, the *Bacchanals*) and is markedly different in quality
  and technique from the E-series. The misattribution entered the literature through an
  18th-century manuscript note and was repeated until the 20th century
  [@web_mantegna_wp; @dummett1980].
- **The programme**: not a tarot in any game-playing sense — the 50 cards do not map
  onto tarot suits or the standard trump hierarchy. They are a **didactic series in the
  tradition of medieval cosmic hierarchies** (estates of man, liberal arts, planetary
  spheres), closer to an illustrated encyclopaedia than a card game.

items.name: Master of the Sola Busca (anonymous engraver, c. 1491)

sections.Who:
The **Master of the Sola Busca** is the conventional name for the anonymous
**North Italian engraver** who produced the **Sola Busca tarot** (c. 1491) —
the **first known fully pictorial 78-card tarot deck**, meaning every card including
the pip minors carries a narrative scene rather than geometric suit symbols.
Several cards bear the monogram **"MS"** which is presumed to indicate the
engraver's initials, but no historical individual has been securely identified
[@web_sb_wp; @web_sb_ciompi].
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **The "MS" monogram**: unresolved. No engraver with those initials active in Northern
  Italy c. 1491 has been definitively identified in archival records [@web_sb_ciompi].
- **Pamela Colman Smith connection**: strongly supported by circumstantial evidence —
  the British Museum exhibited the Sola Busca's hand-coloured photographs in 1907, two
  years before the RWS was published, and several RWS pip compositions show close
  parallels to Sola Busca cards [@web_pcs_wp; @web_sb_wp]. This is now the
  mainstream scholarly view but was not widely known until the 2000s–2010s.
- **The commissioner**: unknown. The "Sola Busca" name comes from the aristocratic
  Milanese family who owned the deck in the 19th century; they may not have commissioned it.

metadata.image_credit: Michael Dummett (2004), photo Klaus Reisinger / R. S. Kissel — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0

sections.Who:
One of the major analytic philosophers of the 20th century (Wykeham Professor of Logic at
Oxford), Michael Dummett was also the most important modern historian of tarot — not as a
divinatory system but as a **trick-taking card game**. His trilogy is the documentary
baseline this collection defaults to: tarot is a 15th-century Italian game; the
occult/Egyptian/divinatory readings are a learned **overlay imposed after c. 1781**.
sections.Claim vs the record:
Dummett **is** the record here. Where a deck's own tradition claims ancient Egyptian,
Hebrew-kabbalistic, or "suppressed wisdom" origins, this collection states the claim fairly
and then weighs it against Dummett's documentary findings. The collection does **not** treat
him as infallible — later scholars (Depaulis, Pratesi, Vitali) refine dates and the A/B/C
picture — but it treats the burden of proof as his framework sets it.
sections.In this collection:
Not a maker — a **lens**. He is cited across nearly every deck dossier as the document-first
authority (`[@dummett1980]`, `[@decker1996]`, `[@dummett2004]`). He does not "feature" in
card art; he features in the collection's *method*.

metadata.lifespan: fl. Marseille, 18th–early 19th c. (dates contested — see below)

sections.Who:
**Nicolas Conver** is the name signed on the most famous single witness of the **Tarot de
Marseille**: a woodblock-and-stencil pack **dated 1760** and printed in **Marseille**, which
became the canonical **"Type II"** Marseille pattern — the reference layout copied by
French cardmakers for more than a century [@web_camoin_conver1760; @web_vs_conver_heritage;
@dummett1980; @depaulis1984]. His official style was *maître cartier* (master cardmaker) of
Marseille [@web_camoin_conver]. **The catch:** the man documented as "Nicolas Conver,
cartier" in the **BnF authority record lived 1784–1833** — making it physically impossible
for *that* Nicolas Conver to have engraved blocks in 1760 [@web_bnf_conver]. The 1760 date
is therefore either inherited from older blocks, or attaches to an earlier/different Conver;
the standard practice was for cardmakers to buy or inherit pearwood moulds, erase the
previous *cartier*'s name, and substitute their own while **leaving the original date**
[@web_vs_conver_heritage]. So "1760" is a real engraving date; "Nicolas Conver, its author"
is the contested part.
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Claim: "Nicolas Conver engraved the gold-standard tarot in 1760."** **The record:** the
  blocks *are* dated 1760, but the BnF-documented Nicolas Conver (1784–1833) was not yet born
  [@web_bnf_conver]. The Camoin firm's own narrative asserts he engraved them in 1760
  [@web_camoin_conver1760]; the document-minded reading is that he (or his workshop)
  **inherited and re-signed older blocks that kept their 1760 date** [@web_vs_conver_heritage].
  Flag the date as belonging to the *object*, and the *authorship* as contested.
- **Claim: the Conver deck is the "original" Tarot de Marseille.** **The record:** it is the
  most renowned and most-copied witness, but the Marseille *pattern* predates it (Noblet
  c. 1650 is older; Chosson is earlier in the Marseille line) — Conver is canonical, not
  primordial [@dummett1980; @depaulis1984].
sections.In this collection:
Conver is the maker of **`tarot-de-marseille-conver`** and the human anchor for the
repo's already-written Marseille deck dossier (`08-tarot-de-marseille.mdx`). Wherever the
grammar names him, render the date as *"dated 1760"* with the authorship hedge, and
distinguish the **Marseille pattern** (broad type) from the **Conver 1760 edition** (this
specific witness). Cards worth featuring are the signature trumps of the Type-II pattern
(e.g. Le Bateleur, La Maison Dieu, Le Monde), but the per-card pass is owned by the deck
dossier.

metadata.image_credit: Oswald Wirth portrait — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0 (la-rose-bleue.org)

sections.Who:
**Joseph Paul Oswald Wirth** (5 August 1860 – 9 March 1943) was a Swiss-born occultist,
illustrator and Freemason who, under the direction of the Kabbalist **Stanislas de Guaita**,
**drew the first overtly occult tarot deck**: the **22 trumps (Major Arcana only)** of *Les 22
Arcanes du Tarot Kabbalistique*, **1889**. He turned **Éliphas Lévi's** tarot–Hebrew–Kabbalah
program into actual *cards*, and decades later issued a revised, far better-known version
alongside his book *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge* (**1927**). He is the Continental
counterpart to the Golden Dawn's English work, and the bridge from Lévi's text to the modern
esoteric deck [@web_wirth_wp; @decker2002; @web_harrington_wirth].
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Myth: the deck *restores* an ancient symbolic tarot.** **The record:** the deck is an
  **honest construction**. Wirth openly builds a *new* symbolic system **on top of the
  Marseille**, following **Lévi's 1850s** Hebrew-letter scheme — itself a 19th-century invention.
  Unlike Gébelin's flat-false Egyptian *antiquity* claim, the Wirth deck is transparent about
  being a designed esoteric overlay; the grammar should record it as a **well-documented
  construction (1889 / 1926)**, not a recovered tradition [@decker2002; @web_wirth_wp].
- **Caveat on the correspondence.** Wirth's Hebrew-letter / trump assignments are **Lévi's
  Continental scheme**, which **differs** from the **Golden Dawn's** English system. Do **not**
  conflate the two correspondence systems [@decker2002; @web_wirth_wp].
- **Two distinct editions.** The **1889** de-Guaita-directed deck and Wirth's **1926** mature
  revision are different objects (e.g. the revised La Papesse) and should be distinguished in
  the grammar [@web_harrington_wirth; @web_wirth_wp].
sections.In this collection:
Wirth is the maker of the deck slug **`oswald-wirth-tarot`** (1889 / 1926, **22 trumps only**).
He should feature on the trumps where his esoteric overlay is most visible — the **Magician**
(alchemical/Hermetic emblems), **La Papesse** (the card he most revised between editions), the
**Hebrew-lettered** trumps generally, and the **Wheel of Fortune** (Lévi's ROTA program made
visual). In a "people of tarot" set he is **"the man who first drew the occult tarot,"** the
illustrator who turned Lévi's theory into cards under de Guaita's direction.

metadata.image_credit: Pamela Colman Smith (1904), The Brooklyn Daily Eagle — Wikimedia Commons (public domain)

sections.Who:
**Pamela Colman Smith** (16 February **1878** – 18 September **1951**), nicknamed **"Pixie,"** was
the artist who **illustrated all 78 cards of the Rider-Waite-Smith tarot** (1909). Her decisive
innovation was the **fully scenic Minor Arcana**: where earlier packs left the pips as bare
arrangements of suit-signs, Smith drew **narrative scenes with figures** on every numbered card —
the move that made the deck readable at a glance and set the template for most modern tarot. For
nearly a century she went **uncredited**, the deck called simply "Rider-Waite"; restoring her name
is one of the clearest documented cases of an erased maker in tarot's record
[@web_pcs_wp; @web_rws_wopc; @web_pcs_artnet].
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **The erasure to correct: "Rider-Waite" leaves Smith out.** She received a **flat fee — and a
  small one — instead of any share of copyright or royalties**, **no formal credit** on the deck or
  packaging, and was written out of its fame for most of a century while the publisher (Rider) and
  the scheme's author (Waite) were named. The collection's use of **Rider-Waite-*Smith*** is the
  deliberate correction; her name belongs on the art [@web_pcs_wp; @web_pcs_artnet].
- **Precision on division of labour:** Waite set the symbolic programme; **Smith drew the cards**,
  and on the Minor Arcana she **invented** as much as she illustrated. The intuitive imagery a modern
  reader "reads" is largely Smith's design choice [@web_rws_wopc].
sections.In this collection:
In `golden-dawn-book-t-tarot`, the rendered imagery is **Smith's RWS art**, so she should feature
prominently — and **specifically on the Minor Arcana (pip) cards**, since her fully scenic pips are
her signal innovation and the reason the deck reads the way it does. The grammar **does** expose
per-card ids, so she is linked in `features_cards` to representative pips —
`golden-dawn-book-t-tarot:wands-02`, `:cups-03`, `:swords-03`, `:swords-10`, `:pentacles-08`
(the suit-prefixed pip ids: `wands-02`…`wands-10`, `cups-02`…, `swords-02`…, `pentacles-02`…).
A "people of tarot" leaf should name her **the artist who gave tarot its pictures** — and note that
she was long denied the credit.

metadata.image_credit: Petrarch by Andrea del Castagno — Wikimedia Commons (public domain)

sections.Who:
**Francesco Petrarca** (Petrarch) was the defining figure of early Italian humanism —
poet, scholar, collector of ancient manuscripts, and the author of the *Trionfi*
(*Triumphs*), an allegorical poem sequence whose **procession of allegorical figures**
is almost certainly the **primary literary source** for the tarot major arcana
[@moakley1966; @dummett1980; @web_petrarch_wp].

Petrarch had no connection to tarot cards, which emerged roughly 50 years after his
death. But his *Trionfi* created the **cultural grammar** — a hierarchical parade of
cosmic forces in which higher powers capture and defeat lower ones — that the tarot
trump designers translated into visual form.
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **The claim**: Petrarch "invented tarot."
- **The correct framing**: Petrarch provided the **literary framework** — the allegoric
  triumph procession with a fixed hierarchy of cosmic forces — that tarot trump designers
  adapted. He did not design or know about tarot cards. The tarot is a *reception* of
  his allegory, not his creation.
- **The scholarly consensus** (Moakley, Dummett, Kaplan): the *Trionfi* connection is
  well-supported and explains both the word *trionfi* for trump cards and the thematic
  logic of the major arcana sequence. But tarot also incorporated **Christian allegory,
  astrological symbolism, and court-game traditions** not found in Petrarch — it was not
  a simple translation of the poem.

sections.Who:
**Pierre Madenié** was a Dijon cardmaker whose **1709 Tarot de Marseille** (complete
deck, signed and dated) is one of the **earliest intact surviving examples** of the
Tarot de Marseille pattern — the woodcut-engraved, stencil-coloured printed tarot that
became the standard European form. The Madenié deck is a critical document in the history
of **TdM standardisation**: it shows the trump iconography in a relatively early form,
predating the 1760 Conver deck that the collection uses as the canonical TdM reference
[@web_tdm_wp; @web_tdm_wopc; @dummett1980].
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **"Nicolas Conver invented the Tarot de Marseille."** **The record**: Conver's 1760 deck
  *standardised* an already-existing iconographic tradition. Madenié's 1709 deck shows
  that the TdM pattern was already stable fifty years earlier. Conver's version became
  canonical because of its quality and because 20th-century occultists (Papus, then the
  entire esoteric tradition) reprinted from it — not because he invented it.

sections.Who:
Stuart Kaplan founded **U.S. Games Systems** (1968) and, more than anyone, drove tarot's
late-20th-century popular revival in the United States. As a collector-cataloguer he compiled
the four-volume **_Encyclopedia of Tarot_**, the most comprehensive descriptive catalogue of
historical decks; as a publisher he reissued the **Rider-Waite-Smith** deck and many others.
sections.Claim vs the record:
Kaplan is a **catalogue** authority more than an origins theorist; his Encyclopedia is
descriptive and is used here for *what a deck is and where copies are*, not for contested
origin claims (for those, defer to the Dummett spine). His commercial framing sometimes
carries the occult tradition's self-description — read those passages as tradition, not as
documented history.
sections.In this collection:
Not a historical maker — a **cataloguer/publisher**. Cited where a dossier needs deck
identification, provenance of surviving copies, or the modern publication history of the RWS
line.

metadata.lifespan: fl. Brussels, c.1762–1803 (firm's active dates; the tarot c.1770/1780)

sections.Who:
**F. I. Vandenborre** was a **Brussels** card-manufacturer whose name attaches to the
standard **Belgian / Flemish tarot** (*Tarot Flamand*), conventionally dated **c. 1780**
(sources give 1770 or 1780; the firm was active **c. 1762–1803**) [@web_wopc_vandenborre;
@web_belgian_wp]. The deck is famous for its **anticlerical substitutions**: card II, the
**Popess (La Papesse)**, is replaced by **the Spanish Captain** (*Le Spagnol Capitano
Eracasse* / Captain Fracasse), and card V, the **Pope (Le Pape)**, by **Bacchus** (*Bacus*),
the god of wine astride a barrel [@web_wopc_vandenborre; @web_th_spanish_captain]. Like the
Marseille deck it has illustrated trumps and **pip** minor cards [@web_wopc_vandenborre].
Vandenborre is a **firm/imprint** more than a richly documented individual; treat as a
maker-node (confidence: medium).
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Claim: Vandenborre "created" the Belgian tarot.** **The record:** he **published and
  standardised** an existing **Rouen-Brussels** pattern; the type predates the firm (Viéville
  c. 1650; Bodet 1693) [@web_belgian_wp; @web_th_rouen_bruxelles].
- **The substitutions are pattern features, not Vandenborre inventions.** The
  **Bacchus/Spanish-Captain** replacements belong to the Belgian tradition this deck carries,
  not uniquely to Vandenborre's hand; they are usually read as a **secular/anticlerical**
  softening of the two ecclesiastical trumps (the Popess being theologically awkward, the Pope
  politically so), echoing the Besançon line's Jupiter/Juno swap for the same two cards
  [@web_th_spanish_captain; @web_belgian_wp]. (confidence: medium on the *motive* — the swap is
  documented; the precise reason is interpretive.)
sections.In this collection:
Vandenborre is the maker of **`belgian-tarot`**. The two cards to feature are exactly the
substitutions: **II — the Spanish Captain** (replacing the Popess) and **V — Bacchus**
(replacing the Pope) — the clearest single illustration in the collection of how a regional
pattern could **edit the ecclesiastical trumps out**. Link the deck back to `jacques-vieville`
(its tradition's ancestor) and contrast it with the Marseille makers.

items.name: The Visconti & Sforza of Milan (ducal patrons)

metadata.lifespan: Filippo Maria Visconti 1392–1447; Francesco Sforza 1401–1466; Bianca Maria Visconti 1425–1468

sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Claim: a "secret" or esoteric origin for these cards.** **The record:** this is
  **conspicuous dynastic display**, not clandestine ritual — the patrons' **heraldry is
  literally painted on the cards**, and the figures are standard Italian Christian-allegorical
  types. They were a **game** (*trionfi*) at court, not a divinatory instrument; any occult
  reading is a much later (18th–19th c.) overlay [@web_vs_wp; @web_wopc_vs; @dummett1980].
- **Claim: the deck commemorates the 1441 wedding.** **The record:** this is the
  **most-discussed theory** for the Cary-Yale specifically, and a reasonable one given a
  gold-leaf pack as a dynastic gift — but it is a hypothesis, and the various Visconti-Sforza
  sets span c. 1441–1460; treat exact occasions as proposed, not proven
  [@web_beinecke_visconti; @web_vs_wp].
sections.In this collection:
This node is the **patron anchor** for the two earliest deck slugs,
**`cary-yale-visconti-tarot`** and **`visconti-sforza-tarot`**. They never appear as "makers"
(`made: []`); in the grammar they are the **commissioners** whose heraldry and dynastic story
explain *why* the decks exist and *how* they are dated. Cards that carry Visconti/Sforza
heraldic devices, and any trumps modelled on family members in period dress, are where this
patron node is most visible.

sections.Who:
**William Rider & Son** was the London publishing house that issued the **Rider-Waite-Smith tarot
deck in 1909** and **A. E. Waite's *Pictorial Key to the Tarot* (1910/1911)** — the "**Rider**" in
the deck's tripartite name. In the early twentieth century it was **the foremost British publisher
of occult, mystical and esoteric literature**, and its commercial release of the Waite–Smith deck
is the moment esoteric tarot crossed from the secret lodge into the open marketplace. The firm
**commissioned neither the artwork nor the symbolic scheme** — those were Smith's and Waite's; its
contribution was publication and distribution [@web_rws_wopc; @web_rider_wp; @web_pkt_wp].
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **The name problem.** Calling the deck "**Rider**-Waite" foregrounds the *publisher* and the
  *scheme-author* while omitting the **artist, Pamela Colman Smith**, who drew all 78 cards. The
  collection's preferred **Rider-Waite-Smith** restores her; "Rider" should be read as the
  *publisher's* mark, not a creative credit [@web_rws_wopc; @web_pcs_wp].
- **Copyright caution.** Rider's original UK copyrights have lapsed in many jurisdictions (UK public
  domain), but the situation is **uneven internationally** and later editions/reissues complicate it
  — keep the repo's "reference, don't necessarily reproduce" caution [@web_rws_faq].
sections.In this collection:
Rider is an **institution node**: the publisher that put esoteric tarot on sale. In any RWS-derived
context (including the `golden-dawn-book-t-tarot` grammar's borrowed imagery), Rider should be named
as the **publisher of record** for the 1909 deck and the *Pictorial Key* — explicitly distinct from
the *maker* (Smith) and the *scheme's author* (Waite), and used to anchor the deck's date and
copyright story.

metadata.image_credit: William Wynn Westcott (photo before 1897) — Wikimedia Commons (public domain)

sections.Who:
**Dr. William Wynn Westcott** (17 December **1848** – 30 July **1925**) was a London coroner,
Freemason and Rosicrucian who, with **Mathers** and **Woodman**, co-founded the Hermetic Order
of the Golden Dawn in 1888. His distinctive contribution was documentary, not artistic: he
brought the **Cipher Manuscripts** to light and decoded them, and produced the supposed
authorising correspondence with the German adept **Anna Sprengel** — the warrant that gave the
Order its Continental pedigree, and which later scholarship holds he **fabricated**. He designed
**no deck** and is not a maker of tarot cards [@web_westcott_wp; @decker1996; @web_gd_swap].
sections.Claim vs the record:
- **Myth: the Sprengel correspondence proves an authentic German Rosicrucian descent.** **The
  record:** while the Cipher Manuscripts themselves are genuine documents, the **Sprengel letters
  are a fabrication** — in 1900 Mathers publicly stated that Westcott had forged them, and the
  scholarly consensus (Decker, Depaulis, Dummett) agrees the page and the correspondence were
  invented to manufacture a lineage [@decker1996; @web_westcott_wp].
- **Precision: Westcott is the Order's founder-administrator, not a tarot maker.** Any tarot
  authority attributed to him belongs properly to Mathers' Book T [@web_mathers_wp].
sections.In this collection:
Westcott is a **context node** for the `golden-dawn` institution: the co-founder whose Cipher
Manuscripts and forged warrant explain *how* the Order came to exist and *why* its claimed
antiquity is a fiction. He should appear in the founding narrative, explicitly **not** credited
with any card or deck.
